title: Roll Your Own Brain: The Self-Hosted AI Server Hustle
goal: Build a self-hosted AI home server with local LLMs and Docker
metaDescription: Roll Your Own Brain: The Self-Hosted AI Server Hustle: a intermediate guide to build a self-hosted ai home server with local llms and docker with clear exp

roadmap.title: Roll Your Own Brain: The Self-Hosted AI Server Hustle

modules.title: Iron Before Ignorance: Scoring the Right Rig
modules.description: Pick hardware that actually runs models, not a space heater that cries
objectives: Identify GPU vs CPU trade-offs for local inference without blowing your rent money
Calculate VRAM requirements based on model sizes you actually want to run
Source used enterprise gear and consumer cards without getting scammed
Plan power supply, cooling, and storage tiers for a rig that runs 24/7

modules.title: Bare Metal or Bust: OS Foundation
modules.description: Strip a machine to its bones and install a real operating system
objectives: Install a headless Linux distro optimized for server workloads
Configure SSH access so you never need a monitor plugged in again
Set up non-root users, sudoers, and basic system hardening from day one
Establish update schedules and kernel parameters for GPU passthrough if needed

modules.title: Containerize or Capitulate: Docker Domination
modules.description: Learn Docker cold or watch your server become an unmanageable mess
objectives: Master Docker core concepts: images, containers, volumes, and networks
Write Dockerfiles and docker-compose.yml files from scratch without copy-pasting
Understand port mapping, volume binding, and environment variable injection
Debug container failures using logs, exec, and inspect like a forensic analyst

modules.title: The Engine Room: Local LLM Runners
modules.description: Deploy the inference engines that make models actually talk
objectives: Install and configure Ollama as a Docker container with GPU acceleration
Compare Ollama vs vLLM vs text-generation-webui and pick the right tool per use case
Expose model APIs over OpenAI-compatible endpoints for other services to consume
Benchmark tokens-per-second across engines and document what your hardware can handle

modules.title: Model Wrangling: Pick, Quantize, Deploy
modules.description: Choose models that fit your hardware and squeeze every drop of performance
objectives: Understand GGUF, AWQ, GPTQ, and EXL2 quantization formats and when each matters
Navigate Hugging Face and Ollama registries to find models worth your VRAM
Calculate memory footprint of quantized models before downloading 40GB you cannot use
Test model quality degradation across quantization levels with real prompts

modules.title: Face of the Beast: Frontend UIs
modules.description: Bolt on a chat interface so you are not talking to a terminal like a caveman
objectives: Deploy Open WebUI as a Docker container connected to your Ollama backend
Configure multi-user access, model switching, and system prompts in the UI
Set up LibreChat as an alternative frontend with OpenAI-compatible API routing
Customize themes, document upload settings, and conversation export features

modules.title: Wires and Tunnels: Network Access
modules.description: Reach your server from anywhere without exposing it to every script kiddie on Earth
objectives: Set up a reverse proxy with Caddy or Nginx for clean subdomain routing
Configure Tailscale or WireGuard for secure remote access without port forwarding
Understand local DNS resolution and split-horizon DNS for internal service names
Implement SSL termination so your self-hosted traffic is encrypted end to end

modules.title: Lock It Down or Get Clowned
modules.description: Harden every layer before someone turns your server into a crypto miner
objectives: Configure UFW or iptables rules with default-deny and explicit allowlists
Set up fail2ban or crowdsec to ban IPs brute-forcing your exposed services
Implement proper secrets management using Docker secrets or .env files with restricted permissions
Audit container privileges and disable root access in every service that allows it

modules.title: Data Vaults: Persistent Storage Strategy
modules.description: Design a storage architecture that survives container nukes and redeploys
objectives: Map out named volumes vs bind mounts and know when to use each
Structure a directory hierarchy for models, configs, databases, and user data
Set up automated backups of persistent data to a secondary disk or NAS
Plan storage capacity growth as model libraries and conversation histories expand

modules.title: Give It a Memory: RAG and Vector Databases
modules.description: Stop hallucinations cold by wiring up retrieval-augmented generation
objectives: Deploy a vector database like ChromaDB or Qdrant as a Docker service
Install embedding models locally and understand chunking strategies for documents
Connect Open WebUI or LibreChat RAG pipelines to your vector store
Test retrieval accuracy with your own documents and tune chunk size and overlap

modules.title: Pulse Check: Monitoring and Health
modules.description: Know exactly when your server is choking before it dies at 3 AM
objectives: Deploy Uptime Kuma or Grafana plus Prometheus for service and system monitoring
Set up GPU monitoring with nvidia-smi logging and container resource stats
Configure alerting through Discord, Telegram, or email for critical failures
Create dashboards showing VRAM usage, token throughput, and disk space trends

modules.title: Autopilot: Compose, Update, Repeat
modules.description: Turn your sprawl of containers into a reproducible, self-healing stack
objectives: Consolidate all services into a single docker-compose stack with proper dependency ordering
Set up Watchtower or Diun for automated image updates with rollback strategies
Create systemd services to ensure Docker and critical containers restart on reboot
Document your entire setup in a Git repo so a full rebuild takes one command, not a weekend

modules.title: Iron Before Ignorance: Scoring the Right Rig
modules.content:
Picture this: you just dropped $2,400 on a tricked-out gaming PC. RGB lights dancing like a damn disco. 64 gigs of RAM. Top-of-the-line processor. You fire up your first local LLM, hit enter, and watch your beautiful beast choke so hard it begs for mercy. The model spits out one word every six seconds. You could read the entire dictionary in the time it takes this thing to generate a paragraph.

Welcome to the consequences of being hardware-ignorant, you dumb beautiful bastard. Pull up a chair. We're fixing your brain before you touch a single credit card.

You're about to build a self-hosted AI home server. Not a gaming rig. Not a NAS that occasionally transcodes a movie. A goddamn inference engine that thinks. And if you get the iron wrong on day one? Every single chapter after this is you trying to polish a turd. I love you too much to let you do that to yourself. So we're going deep on hardware. By the end of this, you'll know exactly what to buy, what to avoid like a radioactive ex, and why your current "gaming PC" might be the worst possible starting point for this project.

## Core Carnage (Rip Apart the Essentials)

### The GPU Cult: Why Your Graphics Card Is the Only Thing That Matters

Here's the truth that hurts: for running local LLMs, your CPU is a glorified paperweight. Your RAM is a backup plan. Your GPU? That's the entire ballgame.

Let me explain why before you start whining about your Ryzen 9.

Back in the stone age of AI (like, 2012), researchers used CPUs for everything. Then some absolute psychopaths at NVIDIA realized that the parallel processing power needed to render video game graphics was mathematically identical to the matrix multiplication needed for neural networks. A CPU has maybe 8 to 24 cores. Each one is a brilliant scholar who can solve complex problems sequentially. A GPU has thousands of cores. Each one is a dumb little worker ant that can only do basic math—but it can do thousands of calculations simultaneously.

LLMs are, at their core, just massive chains of matrix multiplication. Billions of parameters multiplied against each other, token by token. 

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Buying a 24-core Threadripper and skipping the dedicated GPU because "more cores = more power." I've watched guys drop three grand on a CPU and then ask why a 7B model runs slower than their grandma typing with mittens. CPUs process tokens sequentially. GPUs process them in parallel. For LLMs, parallel wins. Every. Single. Time.

Now, here's where it gets brutal. Not all GPUs are created equal. And I don't mean speed—I mean architecture.

NVIDIA owns this space. Not because they're the good guys (they're not, they charge like they're selling donor organs), but because they built CUDA. CUDA is a parallel computing platform that lets developers talk directly to NVIDIA GPUs. Almost every major AI framework—PyTorch, TensorFlow, vLLM, half the tools we'll use in this book—was built on CUDA. 

AMD is out here fighting the good fight with ROCm, and I genuinely root for them, but it's still a buggy mess on Linux for half the AI stack. Intel? Adorable. Maybe in three years.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** If you're building an AI server in 2024, you buy NVIDIA. You won't like the price. You'll like the alternative (nothing works) even less. This isn't brand loyalty—it's ecosystem survival.

### VRAM: The Only Number That Actually Matters

You think I'm exaggerating. I'm not.

VRAM (Video RAM) is the memory on your GPU. It's where the model lives while it's running. And here's the rule that will save you thousands of dollars:

**If the model doesn't fit in VRAM, it doesn't run. Or it runs so slow you'll age visibly waiting for a response.**

Let me break down the math like we're on a bar napkin.

An LLM is quantified by parameter count. A "7B" model has 7 billion parameters. Each parameter is, in its natural state, a 16-bit floating point number (FP16). That's 2 bytes per parameter.

7 billion parameters × 2 bytes = 14,000,000,000 bytes ≈ 14 GB.

So a 7B model in its natural, uncompressed state needs 14 GB of VRAM just to sit there. Plus another 1-2 GB for the "context window" (the conversation history you're feeding it). You're at 16 GB minimum.

But here's where it gets beautiful. A technique called **quantization**—pioneered by researchers trying to squeeze models onto phones—lets us compress those 16-bit numbers into 4-bit or even 3-bit numbers. You lose a tiny bit of accuracy (we're talking like 1-2% degradation in quality) but you cut the memory requirement by 75%.

At 4-bit quantization (which is what 90% of the local AI community uses):
- 7B model → ~4 GB VRAM
- 13B model → ~8 GB VRAM  
- 34B model → ~20 GB VRAM
- 70B model → ~40 GB VRAM

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** The model size in billions × 0.7 (for 4-bit quant) gives you a rough VRAM estimate in gigabytes. Add 2 GB for context. That's your minimum. Want to run a 70B model? You need roughly 40+ GB of VRAM. No, you can't split that across two cheap cards without a performance hit. Yes, I'll explain why later.

Still breathing? Good. Because this next part separates the pretenders from the players.

### The VRAM Tier List: What Card Buys You What Life

Let's get concrete. Here's what different NVIDIA cards get you in the local LLM world:

**8 GB VRAM (RTX 3060 Ti, 4060, used 2070 Super):**
You can run 7B models comfortably. This is the "starter kit." You'll have a smart, capable assistant that's roughly GPT-3.5 level. Good enough for coding help, writing drafts, asking questions. You will outgrow this in three months. I guarantee it.

**12 GB VRAM (RTX 3060 12GB, 4070):**
The sweet spot for beginners who want breathing room. 7B models fly. 13B models fit. This is the "I'm serious about this but I also have rent" tier. The RTX 3060 12GB is the single best value card in existence for AI right now. Used ones go for $250-300. 

**16 GB VRAM (RTX 4060 Ti 16GB):**
Uncomfortable middle child. Better than 12GB, not as good as 24GB. 13B models breathe easy. You can squeeze in smaller 34B models with aggressive quantization. 

**24 GB VRAM (RTX 3090, 4090):**
The holy grail for consumer AI. A used RTX 3090 is the undisputed king of budget AI. You can run 34B models with full context. You can run 70B models with 3-bit quantization (barely). These cards are the reason the local AI community exists. $700-900 used. Worth every penny.

**48 GB+ VRAM (RTX 6000 Ada, A6000, A100):**
Enterprise gear. We're talking $4,000-15,000. If you're buying this for a home server, you either have a trust fund or a very specific professional need. We'll talk about how to source these without getting scammed, but most of you should stop at the 3090.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** The RTX 3060 12GB is the card I recommend to 80% of people starting out. It's cheap, it's reliable, and 12GB lets you actually do interesting things. The other 20% should buy a used 3090 and never look back. Everything in between is a compromise that satisfies no one.

### The CPU: Your Dignified Backup Plan

Here's where I eat some of my own words. The CPU DOES matter—just not for inference speed. It matters for:

1. **Running the server itself.** Docker, the OS, your networking stack—all CPU.
2. **Pre-processing.** When you feed documents into a RAG system (Chapter 10), the CPU chunks and processes that data before the GPU ever sees it.
3. **Fallback inference.** If your GPU is busy or you're running a model that doesn't fit in VRAM, the CPU takes over. It's slow. Painfully slow. But it works.

For a home AI server, you want something with 8+ cores and decent single-thread performance. The good news? Almost any modern CPU from the last 4-5 years handles this fine. A used Ryzen 7 5700X for $120 will do the job. An Intel i5-12400 for $130. Don't overthink this.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Spending $500+ on a CPU and then buying an 8GB GPU. Flip that ratio. Your GPU should cost 2-3x what your CPU costs. If it doesn't, you're building a gaming PC, not an AI server.

### RAM: The Safety Net

Here's the rule: your system RAM should be at least 2x your VRAM, minimum 32GB.

Why? Because when you load a model, it goes from your storage → system RAM → VRAM. If you have a 24GB GPU running a 20GB model, you need at least 20GB of system RAM free just to hold the model in transit. Plus your OS overhead. Plus Docker. Plus whatever else is running.

32GB is the floor. 64GB is comfortable. 128GB is "I never want to think about this again." DDR4 is fine. DDR5 is nicer but not essential. Don't blow your budget here.

### Storage: The Three-Tier System

This is where most builds faceplant into a ditch. You need three types of storage, and if you cheap out on any of them, your server becomes a misery machine.

**Tier 1: The Boot Drive (NVMe SSD, 500GB-1TB)**
This holds your OS, Docker images, and active model files. Speed matters here because loading a 20GB model from a slow drive is like watching paint dry in a coma. NVMe SSDs hit 3,000-7,000 MB/s read speeds. A Samsung 970 Evo Plus, a WD Black SN850X, a Crucial P3 Plus—any of these will do. Budget $60-100.

**Tier 2: The Model Vault (SATA SSD or large NVMe, 2-4TB)**
Model files (called GGUF files, which we'll cover in Chapter 5) are massive. A single 70B model can be 40GB. You'll collect these like Pokémon. You need space. SATA SSDs are cheaper per terabyte and fast enough for storage. Budget $150-250.

**Tier 3: The Cold Storage (HDD, 4TB+)**
For documents, datasets, backups, and models you're keeping but not actively running. A spinning rust drive. Slow as hell but cheap per terabyte. $80-120.

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Never run models from an HDD. I tried it once "just to see." A model that loads in 8 seconds from NVMe took 4 minutes from an HDD. Four. Minutes. Of my life. Gone. Learn from my mistakes.

### Power Supply: The Heart You Don't Want to Skimp On

Your AI server will run 24/7. It will pull power constantly. If your PSU dies, it can take your GPU, your motherboard, and your dreams with it.

Here's the math on power draw:
- RTX 3090: 350W under load
- RTX 3060 12GB: 170W under load
- CPU: 65-105W under load
- Motherboard, RAM, drives, fans: ~50-80W

For a single 3090 build: you need a minimum 850W PSU. 1000W gives you headroom. For a dual-GPU setup (two 3090s): 1200W minimum, 1600W recommended.

Buy from a reputable brand (Corsair, Seasonic, EVGA). Get an 80+ Gold rating minimum. Platinum is better. Don't even look at Bronze. This is not the component to save $40 on.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Buying a cheap no-name PSU because "watts is watts." No. No they are not. Cheap PSUs have dirty power delivery, voltage spikes, and no protection circuits. When (not if) they fail, they can fry every component in your build. I've seen it happen. It's heartbreaking. Spend the money.

### Cooling: Your Server Is Now a Space Heater

An RTX 3090 under sustained inference load dumps 350W of heat into your room. That's the equivalent of a small space heater running constantly. In a closed room, the ambient temperature will rise 5-10°F within an hour.

For the GPU itself: the 3090 Founders Edition has a notorious memory temperature problem. The GDDR6X memory modules on the back of the card hit 100°C+ under load and thermal throttle. You need either:
1. An aftermarket cooler (Gigabyte Gaming OC, ASUS TUF versions have better cooling)
2. Active airflow directly on the back of the card (a small fan pointed at it)
3. A water cooling block (expensive, overkill for most)

For the case: you want high airflow. Fractal Design Meshify 2, Lian Li Lancool III, Phanteks Eclipse G500A. Avoid "quiet" cases with sound dampening foam—they trap heat. Your server will not be quiet. Accept this now.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** Your AI server lives in a room. That room gets hot. If it's a small office or bedroom, you need to factor in additional cooling costs. I run my server in a basement. If you don't have a basement, put it near a vent or in a room you can close off. Your electric bill will go up $30-60/month depending on your rates. Budget for this.

## Street Smarts (How to Actually Use This)

### The Build Tiers: Pick Your Fighter

Let me make this dead simple. Three tiers. Pick the one that matches your wallet and your ambition.

**Tier 1: "I'm Dipsh*t But I'm Trying" ($600-800)**
- GPU: Used RTX 3060 12GB ($250-300)
- CPU: Ryzen 5 5600 or Intel i5-12400 ($100-130)
- RAM: 32GB DDR4 ($60)
- Storage: 1TB NVMe + 2TB SATA SSD ($130)
- PSU: 650W Gold ($70)
- Case: Whatever has good airflow ($60-80)
- Motherboard: B550 or B660 ($80-100)

This rig runs 7B models at 30-50 tokens/second. 13B models at 15-20 t/s. You can build a RAG system, run a local chat interface, and learn everything in this book. You will NOT run 70B models. Accept that now and save yourself the disappointment.

**Tier 2: "I'm Serious About This" ($1,200-1,600)**
- GPU: Used RTX 3090 24GB ($700-900)
- CPU: Ryzen 7 5700X or Intel i5-13600K ($150-200)
- RAM: 64GB DDR4 ($120)
- Storage: 1TB NVMe + 4TB SATA SSD + 4TB HDD ($250)
- PSU: 1000W Gold ($130-150)
- Case: High airflow, full ATX ($100)
- Motherboard: B550 or Z690 ($120-150)

This is the build I recommend if you can afford it. The 3090 is a monster. 34B models fly. 70B models run (slowly, but they run). You can experiment with model fine-tuning. You have room to grow. This rig will serve you for 2-3 years minimum.

**Tier 3: "I Have Problems" ($3,000+)**
- Dual RTX 3090s ($1,400-1,800 used)
- Threadripper or high-end Ryzen 9 ($400-600)
- 128GB RAM ($250)
- 2TB NVMe + 8TB SSD + 8TB HDD ($400)
- 1600W Platinum PSU ($200-250)
- Server rack or open-air case ($150-200)

You're running 70B models with full context. You're fine-tuning. You're possibly serving multiple users simultaneously. You have a problem and I respect it. Just make sure your electrical circuit can handle 1000W+ continuous draw—most residential breakers are 15A/120V (1,800W max). You're getting close to that limit.

### Sourcing Used Gear Without Getting Fleeced

The used market for GPUs is a minefield of scammers, miners, and people selling broken cards to unsuspecting rubes. Here's how to navigate it.

**Where to buy:**
- eBay (with buyer protection, filtered by "Sold Items" to see real market prices)
- r/hardwareswap on Reddit (moderated, requires verification)
- Local Facebook Marketplace/Craigslist (risky but no shipping damage)
- Server recycling companies for enterprise gear (ServerMonkey, SaveMyServer)

**What to check on a used GPU:**
1. Ask the seller to run `nvidia-smi` and send a screenshot. This shows the driver version, VRAM, and most importantly—the "Power Draw" and "Temperature" readings. If they refuse, walk away.
2. Ask for a 3DMark Time Spy benchmark score. If the card is degraded from mining, the score will be lower than reference.
3. Look at the card photos. Are the thermal pads intact? Is there visible damage to the PCB? Are the fans clean or caked in dust?
4. For 3090s specifically: ask about memory junction temperatures. If the seller doesn't know what you're talking about, they probably didn't abuse it. If they say "yeah I repadded it," ask for photos of the repad job. A bad repad is worse than stock.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Buying a "mining card" because it's cheap. Mining cards ran 24/7 at optimized power limits. This is actually LESS damaging than gaming (which has thermal cycling). The real danger is cards from failed water cooling loops or physical damage from shipping. A mining card from a clean setup is fine. A card that "was working when removed" with no proof is a gamble.

### The "Mind-Blown" Moment: PCIe Lane Bandwidth

Here's something nobody tells you until you've already f*cked it up.

If you ever go dual-GPU (two 3090s), the PCIe lane configuration matters more than you think. Most consumer motherboards have one full PCIe x16 slot and a second slot that's either x8 or—god help you—x4.

When you run a model split across two GPUs, they need to talk to each other constantly. The data flows GPU1 → CPU → GPU2 through the PCIe bus. If your second slot is x4 (running through the chipset), you're bottlenecking that communication hard.

A PCIe 4.0 x16 slot has 64 GB/s bandwidth. A PCIe 4.0 x4 slot has 16 GB/s. That 4x difference can mean 30-40% slower inference on split models.

If you're planning dual GPUs, you need a motherboard that supports x8/x8 lane splitting. Look for boards with PLX switches or explicit "x8/x8" support in the specs. Threadripper boards handle this natively. Consumer boards are a crapshoot.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** The motherboard is the unsung hero of multi-GPU builds. A $150 motherboard will bottleneck two $800 GPUs. A $400 motherboard will let them sing. Don't skimp on the board if you're going dual-GPU. Check the PCIe lane breakdown in the manual before you buy. If it says "x16 / x4," run.

### Power Circuits: Don't Burn Your House Down

Your AI server pulls power continuously. Unlike a gaming PC that spikes during gameplay and idles the rest of the time, an inference server under load can sustain 500-800W for hours.

Standard US residential circuits are 15 amps at 120 volts. That's 1,800 watts maximum. Your server pulls 800W. Your monitor pulls 50W. Your lights pull 100W. Your phone charger pulls 15W. You're at 965W. Fine.

But if you're on the same circuit as a space heater (1,500W) or a window AC unit (1,000W+), you're going to trip breakers. Or worse—melt an old extension cord and start a fire.

Find out which circuit your server will be on. Turn off the breaker and see what loses power. If it's shared with high-draw appliances, run a new circuit or move the server. This is not optional. This is "don't burn your house down" territory.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** I know a guy who had his AI server on the same circuit as his refrigerator. Every time the compressor kicked on, the voltage dipped and his server rebooted. He lost unsaved work three times before he figured it out. Check your circuits. It takes ten minutes and saves you infinite pain.

### The Final Build Checklist

Before you click "buy" on anything, run through this:

- [ ] GPU VRAM matches the largest model you want to run (×0.7 + 2GB)
- [ ] GPU is NVIDIA (I'm sorry, I'm so sorry, but it has to be)
- [ ] System RAM is at least 2x your VRAM
- [ ] PSU is 80+ Gold or better, from a reputable brand
- [ ] PSU wattage exceeds your max system draw by 20%+
- [ ] NVMe SSD for boot drive and active models
- [ ] Case has high airflow (mesh front, multiple fan mounts)
- [ ] Motherboard PCIe lanes support your GPU configuration
- [ ] You know which electrical circuit the server will live on
- [ ] You have a plan for the heat (basement, separate room, additional cooling)

If you can't check every box, stop. Rethink. Adjust. 

YOU ABSOLUTE LEGEND. You just survived the hardware gauntlet. Most people tap out here. They buy the wrong card, blame the software, and quit. Not you. You beautiful disaster. You now know more about AI server hardware than 90% of the "experts" on YouTube who are telling you to buy a Mac Studio.

Next chapter, we're stripping whatever machine you end up with down to its bones and installing a real operating system. Not Windows. Not macOS. We're going to the bare metal. And it's going to be glorious.

Go buy your iron. I'll be here when you get back.

modules.title: Bare Metal or Bust: OS Foundation
modules.content:
Picture this: it's 2 AM, your model is mid-inference, and your server decides it's a great time to download a 4-gigabyte Windows update, reboot, and lock you out because Microsoft thinks you *really* need to see a screensaver of a waterfall. If you're running your AI rig on Windows, you're not a server admin — you're a tourist with a very expensive space heater.

You dumb beautiful bastard. You survived Chapter 1. You've got the rig, the GPU, the silicon that cost you a month's rent. But right now, that hardware is naked. Exposed. Running whatever bloated commercial OS came pre-installed on it. We're stripping it to the f*cking studs today.

## Core Carnage (Rip Apart the Essentials)

Your operating system is the bouncer between your hardware and your models. If that bouncer is busy updating Candy Crush, your model waits. For a server, that's unacceptable.

### The Headless Mandate

A "headless" server means no GUI. No desktop. No mouse. No pretty little taskbar. Just a black terminal screen and the command line.

Why? Because every cycle your CPU spends rendering a transparent window shadow is a cycle it's NOT spending on inference. A desktop environment eats 1-2 GB of RAM just sitting there, breathing. On a server, that's a sin. You run headless or you run home.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Installing Ubuntu Desktop "just in case I need a GUI later." No. Stop. You will NEVER need that GUI. SSH exists. If you need to see a web page, use the browser on your laptop. Your server is a workhorse, not a display pony.

### The Distro Decision: Ubuntu Server vs. Debian vs. Proxmox

I know what you're thinking: "There are 500 Linux distros, how do I pick?" Cute. Here's the answer: you pick the one that has the most documentation and the largest community, because when (not if) something breaks at 3 AM, you need Stack Overflow to have the answer.

**Ubuntu Server LTS (Long Term Support)** is the reigning champ for beginners. It's the Toyota Corolla of Linux — not exciting, but it starts every morning, parts are everywhere, and everyone knows how to fix it. LTS means 5 years of guaranteed updates. You don't upgrade your OS every 6 months on a production server. That's insane.

**Debian** is Ubuntu's austere, slightly faster grandfather. Ubuntu is built on Debian. Debian is leaner, more stable, and has zero corporate baggage. But it ships with older packages, which means driver headaches if you have cutting-edge GPUs.

**Proxmox** is the wild card. It's not just an OS — it's a Type-1 hypervisor. It runs directly on your bare metal and lets you carve your machine into virtual machines (VMs) and Linux Containers (LXC). If you want to run your AI stack in one VM, a media server in another, and a firewall in a third, Proxmox is your jam.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** For 90% of you reading this, install Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS or 24.04 LTS. Done. Debate over. Don't be the guy who installs Arch Linux on a production server because "I use Arch, btw." Nobody cares. Stability wins. Period.

### The Install: Burn, Boot, Bash

You're going to flash a USB drive with the OS image using a tool like Rufus or balenaEtcher. Boot from it. And then follow the most boring installer in the world.

The key moments in the installer:

1. **Network:** Plug in Ethernet. Wi-Fi on a server is like bringing a squirt gun to a house fire. Don't.
2. **Storage:** Choose "Use Entire Disk" and set up LVM (Logical Volume Management). LVM lets you resize partitions later without pulling your hair out. You'll thank me in six months.
3. **Profile:** Name yourself. Set a password. And for the love of everything holy, **DO NOT lose this password.**
4. **SSH Server:** CHECK THIS BOX. If you don't, you're locked to that physical monitor forever like a caveman staring at a wall. The installer calls it "OpenSSH server." Check it. Tattoo it on your hand.

### SSH: The Lifeline

SSH (Secure Shell) is how you talk to your server from your couch, your bed, or a different country. It's an encrypted tunnel between your laptop and the server. Once it's on, you unplug the monitor, the keyboard, the mouse — everything. The server becomes a headless brick that only you can wake up.

Here's the flow. From your laptop:

```bash
ssh yourusername@192.168.1.50
```

That's it. You're in. You're looking at the server's terminal through your laptop's screen. The monitor is now dead weight.

But here's the thing — every script kiddie on Earth knows SSH runs on port 22. They'll hammer it. We'll harden that in Module 8, but for now, know that SSH is your front door, and the default lock is cheap.

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Set a STATIC IP for your server in your router's DHCP settings. If your server's IP changes every reboot because your router feels spicy, your SSH commands break, your Docker ports break, everything breaks. Static IP. Non-negotiable.

### The Non-Root Commandment

Root is God. Root can delete the entire filesystem with one command. Root has no training wheels, no warnings, no "are you sure?"

You will NOT run as root. You will create a standard user account and give it `sudo` privileges. `sudo` ("superuser do") lets you run commands as root, but it requires your password and logs everything. It's the difference between handing someone the keys to your car and sitting in the passenger seat while they drive.

Here's how you lock it down after install:

```bash
# Create a new user (if you didn't during install)
sudo adduser aiadmin

# Give them sudo powers
sudo usermod -aG sudo aiadmin

# Switch to the new user
su - aiadmin
```

The `sudoers` file controls who gets to use `sudo`. You edit it with `sudo visudo` — and you ALWAYS use `visudo`, never `nano /etc/sudoers`. Why? Because `visudo` checks your syntax before saving. If you corrupt the sudoers file with a typo, you lock yourself out of root entirely. You're bricked. `visudo` prevents that.

### Updates: The Necessary Evil

Your fresh install is already outdated. The kernel, the drivers, the security patches — all stale by the time the ISO hits your USB drive.

```bash
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
```

That's it. `apt update` refreshes the package list. `apt upgrade` installs everything new. The `-y` flag means "yes, stop asking me to confirm every damn package."

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** Set up automatic security updates. You don't need to personally approve every patch at 3 AM. Ubuntu has `unattended-upgrades` for this. But — and this is critical — DO NOT auto-update the kernel or GPU drivers on a production server. A kernel update can break your GPU passthrough or driver compatibility overnight. Auto-update security patches. Manually update kernels. Always.

### Kernel Parameters and GPU Passthrough: The Deep End

Here's where it gets spicy. If you're running Proxmox or any hypervisor, and you want your VM to have direct access to your GPU (which you DO, because your LLM needs every drop of that VRAM), you need **GPU passthrough** (also called IOMMU or VFIO).

Without passthrough, your VM gets a virtual GPU that's about as useful as a calculator for running Llama 3. With passthrough, the VM talks directly to your RTX 3090 like it's the only thing in the room.

This requires kernel-level configuration. You're telling the Linux kernel: "Hey, isolate this GPU from the host OS and reserve it for a virtual machine."

The tech is called **IOMMU (Input/Output Memory Management Unit)**. Intel calls it VT-d. AMD calls it AMD-Vi. Same concept, different branding.

You enable it in your GRUB config:

```bash
# Edit GRUB
sudo nano /etc/default/grub

# Find this line and add the parameters
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash intel_iommu=on iommu=pt"
# (For AMD, use amd_iommu=on)

# Update GRUB
sudo update-grub

# Reboot
sudo reboot
```

After reboot, verify IOMMU is active:

```bash
dmesg | grep -e IOMMU -e DMAR
```

If you see IOMMU groups listed, you're golden. If not, check your BIOS — virtualization features must be enabled there first.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to enable virtualization in BIOS. Your OS can scream `intel_iommu=on` all day long, but if the BIOS has VT-d disabled, nothing happens. It's like screaming at a microwave to bake a cake — wrong layer.

### The Mind-Blower: Your OS Is Not Your Software

Here's the thing that breaks people's brains: the OS doesn't run your models. The OS doesn't care about your LLM. The OS is a traffic cop. It manages memory, schedules CPU time, handles file I/O, and talks to hardware drivers. That's it.

Your models live in containers (Chapter 3). Your inference engines live in containers (Chapter 4). Your UI lives in containers (Chapter 6). The OS is the concrete foundation. You don't drive on the foundation — you build the road on top of it.

This is why we don't need a fancy OS. We need a STABLE one. The fancier the OS, the more ways it can break. Ubuntu Server LTS is boring. Boring is good. Boring means predictable. Predictable means you sleep at night.

## Street Smarts (How to Actually Use This)

Still breathing? Good. Because this next part separates the pretenders from the players.

### The Post-Install Checklist

After your installer finishes and you SSH in for the first time, run this sequence. Tattoo it on your brain:

```bash
# 1. Update everything
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

# 2. Install essential tools
sudo apt install -y curl git wget htop nano build-essential

# 3. Set hostname so you know what machine you're on
sudo hostnamectl set-hostname ai-server

# 4. Verify SSH is running and enabled on boot
sudo systemctl enable ssh
sudo systemctl status ssh

# 5. Check your GPU is visible to the OS
lspci | grep -i nvidia
# (or 'amd' / 'vga' for other setups)
```

If that `lspci` command shows your GPU, the OS sees it. If it doesn't, you've got a hardware issue or a dead PCIe slot. Fix that before anything else.

### SSH Keys: Kill the Password

Passwords are for amateurs. SSH keys are cryptographic pairs — a public key and a private key. The public key goes on the server. The private key stays on your laptop. They handshake, verify each other, and let you in without typing a damn thing.

From your LAPTOP (not the server):

```bash
# Generate the key pair
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "ai-server-key"

# Copy the public key to the server
ssh-copy-id aiadmin@192.168.1.50
```

Now test it:

```bash
ssh aiadmin@192.168.1.50
```

No password prompt. You're in. You beautiful disaster.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** Once you confirm your SSH key works, disable password authentication entirely. Every bot on the planet brute-forces SSH passwords. If passwords are off, they can hammer your door forever and get nothing. Edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`, set `PasswordAuthentication no`, and restart SSH. This single change eliminates 99% of automated attacks.

### The Firewall: Day One

Ubuntu ships with `ufw` (Uncomplicated Firewall). It is exactly what it sounds like. Turn it on:

```bash
# Allow SSH first (OR YOU WILL LOCK YOURSELF OUT)
sudo ufw allow 22/tcp

# Enable the firewall
sudo ufw enable

# Check status
sudo ufw status verbose
```

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Enabling `ufw` before allowing SSH. I've watched people do this. They enable the firewall, get kicked out of their own server, and have to physically drive to the data center with a monitor under their arm. Don't be that guy. Allow SSH FIRST. Always.

### Sudoers: The VIP List

Remember the `sudoers` file? Here's how you tighten it. Open it with:

```bash
sudo visudo
```

Find the line that says:

```
%sudo   ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL
```

This means "anyone in the sudo group can run any command as any user." That's fine for now. But later, if you add service accounts (for Docker, for monitoring agents, etc.), you do NOT give them sudo access. They get specific, limited permissions. Principle of least privilege. A service account with full sudo is a ticking time bomb.

### Kernel Updates: The Landmine

Remember how I said auto-update security patches but manually update kernels? Here's why.

Your GPU driver (NVIDIA CUDA, AMD ROCm) is compiled against a specific kernel version. When the kernel updates, the driver module may not match anymore. Your GPU vanishes. Your containers can't access it. Your models stop working. At 3 AM. On a Tuesday.

The fix: pin your kernel or hold the package.

```bash
# Hold the kernel package from auto-updating
sudo apt-mark hold linux-image-generic linux-headers-generic

# When YOU are ready to update (with testing), unhold:
sudo apt-mark unhold linux-image-generic linux-headers-generic
```

You update kernels on YOUR schedule — when you're awake, when you can test, and when you can fix it if it breaks.

### The Real Reason This Matters

Here's the deal, kid. You're going to spend the next 10 chapters building on top of this OS. Docker containers, LLM runners, vector databases, monitoring stacks — ALL of it sits on this foundation.

If your OS is shaky, everything above it is shaky. If your SSH breaks, you can't manage anything. If your kernel updates and kills your GPU driver, your entire AI stack goes dark. If your firewall is open, some teenager in Estonia turns your $3,000 rig into a crypto miner.

This chapter isn't sexy. Nobody brags about installing Ubuntu Server at a party. But the people who skip this sh*t? They're the ones posting "HELP MY SERVER IS DOWN AND I DON'T KNOW WHY" at 3 AM on Reddit.

You're not that person. You're building this right. Bare metal. Clean OS. Tight permissions. Locked doors.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** The most important skill in server administration isn't knowing every command — it's knowing what NOT to touch. Discipline beats cleverness. The admin who never breaks their server is worth 10x the admin who can fix it fast. Be the first one.

### What You Just Built

Let me paint the picture of where you are right now:

- A machine running a clean, headless Linux server OS
- SSH access from your laptop, no monitor required
- A non-root user with sudo access
- A firewall allowing only SSH traffic
- SSH key authentication, passwords disabled
- A GPU visible to the OS, drivers pending
- A kernel held stable, security patches auto-installing

That's a real server, champ. Not a desktop with ideas above its station. A real, hardened, headless server ready to have Docker screamed onto it.

Unplug the monitor. Put it in the closet. You won't need it again.

Next chapter, we're pouring concrete into this foundation with Docker. You're going to learn why containers replaced bare-metal installs for everything short of the kernel itself, and why the alternative is maintaining dependency hell across a dozen services.

Go get a coffee. You earned it, you dumb beautiful bastard. The real work starts now.

---

modules.title: Containerize or Capitulate: Docker Domination
modules.content:
Picture this: it's 2 AM. Your model just crashed. You SSH into your box — the one you set up in Chapter 2 like a damn champion — and you're staring at seventeen different processes, four broken Python environments, a GPU driver that decided to s*it the bed during an apt upgrade, and dependencies scattered across your filesystem like shrapnel after a pipe bomb. You don't know what broke. You can't undo it. And your model? Dead. Cold. Gone.

That nightmare? That's what life was like BEFORE Docker. Every. Single. Time.

You beautiful bastard, you survived OS installation. You've got a bare metal box running Ubuntu Server LTS, SSH locked down, GPU drivers humming. You should be proud. Now throw all that pride in the trash because if you install your AI stack the way cavemen did — `apt install` this, `pip install` that, `curl | bash` like an animal — you're building a house of cards on a fault line during a f*cking earthquake.

Enter Docker. The container. The single most important tool in modern infrastructure. And by the end of this chapter, you're going to understand it better than 90% of "DevOps engineers" on LinkedIn who put it on their resume because they once ran `docker pull hello-world`.

## Core Carnage (Rip Apart the Essentials)

### Why Docker Exists (The "Works on My Machine" Plague)

Here's a fun fact that'll mess with your head: Docker didn't invent containers. Not even close. Container technology has existed in Linux since 1979 with `chroot`. Solaris Zones came in 2004. LXC — the thing Docker was originally built on top of — landed in 2008. Solomon Hykes and his team at dotCloud (a Platform-as-a-Service company) were using LXC internally and realized they'd built something incredible on top of it. They open-sourced it in 2013 as Docker, and the tech world lost its collective mind.

Why? Because Docker solved the single most expensive, soul-crushing problem in software engineering: **"It works on my machine."**

You know the drill. Developer writes code on their MacBook. It works. They push it to a server. It explodes. Why? Because the server has a different version of libc. Because the Python path is different. Because some a*shole ran `apt upgrade` six months ago and silently broke a dependency. Because the GPU driver on the server doesn't match the CUDA version the code expects. Every single one of these is a 4 AM phone call waiting to happen.

Docker fixes this by packaging your application AND everything it needs — the OS libraries, the runtime, the dependencies, the config files — into a single, immutable artifact called an **image**. That image runs identically on your laptop, your server, your coworker's machine, or a cloud instance. Same behavior. Same dependencies. Same f*king everything.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** An image is a snapshot. A container is a running instance of that snapshot. Think of it like a class and an object in programming — the image is the blueprint, the container is the living, breathing thing that's actually doing work.

### Images: The Blueprint

An image is a read-only, layered filesystem. Here's what nobody explains to you properly: images aren't single files. They're a stack of layers, each one representing a change to the filesystem. It's like git commits for an entire operating system.

When you write a Dockerfile and say `FROM ubuntu:22.04`, you're pulling a base layer — just the Ubuntu filesystem. When you say `RUN apt-get install python3`, Docker creates a NEW layer on top with Python installed. When you `COPY` your app code in, that's another layer. Stack them all together and you get the final image.

The brilliant part? **Layers are shared.** If you and I both build images from `ubuntu:22.04`, we both download that base layer once. If we both install Python, we might share that layer too. This is why Docker images are shockingly small to download after your first few — you're just pulling the diffs.

### Containers: The Living Thing

A container is what happens when you take an image and say "go." Docker takes that read-only image, slaps a writable layer on top (called the container layer), allocates some resources, and starts a process. That process thinks it has its own machine. It has its own filesystem, its own network, its own process tree. But it's NOT a virtual machine.

Here's the mind-blown moment you weren't expecting: **containers don't contain an operating system kernel.** A VM runs a full guest kernel — that's why VMs are heavy and slow to boot. A container uses YOUR host kernel. The container just sees a restricted view of it through Linux namespaces and cgroups. Namespaces isolate what the container can see (process IDs, network interfaces, mount points). Cgroups limit what it can use (CPU, memory, GPU access).

This is why a container boots in milliseconds while a VM takes thirty seconds. There's no boot sequence. There's no BIOS. There's no kernel initialization. Docker just creates some namespaces, forks a process, and boom — you're running. It's not magic, it's just Linux being clever as hell.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** People think containers are "lightweight VMs." They are NOT. If your host kernel is 5.15 and your container needs kernel 6.0 features, you're screwed. The container shares the host kernel. Period. This is also why you can't run Linux containers natively on Windows without a translation layer — different kernels entirely.

### Volumes: Don't Lose Your Sh*t

Here's where rookies nuke themselves. Remember that writable layer on top of the image? The container layer? **It dies when the container dies.** You stop the container, remove it, recreate it from the image — every file that process wrote to disk is GONE. Vaporized. Like it never existed.

Imagine your AI model downloads 40 GB of weights, saves them to its filesystem, you restart the container, and now it has to download them again. At 2 AM. On a 50 Mbps connection. While you weep.

That's what **volumes** are for. A volume is a Docker-managed storage location that exists OUTSIDE the container's lifecycle. The container writes to it like a normal directory, but Docker keeps it safe on the host filesystem. Kill the container, nuke it, rebuild it — the volume persists. Your model weights stay right where they are.

There are two types you care about:
- **Named volumes:** Docker manages them. You give them a name like `model-cache`, Docker puts them somewhere on disk (usually `/var/lib/docker/volumes/`), and you don't think about it. Good for data where you don't need to know the exact path.
- **Bind mounts:** You specify an exact path on the host — like `/home/you/models:/app/models` — and Docker maps it directly into the container. Good for config files, development, and when you need to access the data from the host too.

### Networks: Containers Need to Talk

By default, Docker gives every container a virtual network interface with its own IP. Containers on the same Docker network can talk to each other by container name. Not IP — NAME. Docker has a built-in DNS resolver that handles this. So if your frontend container needs to reach your LLM backend container, it just calls `http://llm-backend:8080` and Docker routes it. No hardcoded IPs. No bulls*it.

This matters enormously for your AI stack. You'll have a frontend UI, an inference engine, maybe a vector database for RAG (we'll get there in Chapter 10), and they ALL need to communicate. Docker networks make this trivial. Put them on the same network, reference by name, done.

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Docker has several network drivers, but you only care about two right now: `bridge` (the default — containers get an internal IP and can talk to each other) and `host` (the container uses the host's network directly, no isolation). For AI inference with GPU passthrough, you'll often want `host` networking to avoid port-mapping overhead — but it's less secure. We'll cover this trade-off in Chapter 8.

## Street Smarts (How to Actually Use This)

### Writing a Dockerfile From Scratch (Stop Copy-Pasting)

A Dockerfile is a recipe. It's a list of instructions that Docker reads top-to-bottom to build an image. Let's write one for a simple Python AI app and I'll explain every single line like you're five.

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .

RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY . .

EXPOSE 8000

CMD ["python", "server.py"]
```

Line by line, you magnificent rookie:

**`FROM python:3.11-slim`** — Start with a base image. `python:3.11-slim` is the official Python image stripped down to essentials. It's smaller than the full image (which includes build tools you don't need). For AI workloads, you might use `nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04` as your base instead — this gives you CUDA libraries without making you install them manually. Choose your base image wisely; it determines your entire foundation.

**`WORKDIR /app`** — Set the working directory inside the container. Every subsequent command runs from here. If `/app` doesn't exist, Docker creates it. This is like `cd /app` but it actually persists.

**`COPY requirements.txt .`** — Copy JUST the requirements file from your host into the container. Not everything. JUST this file. Why? Because of layer caching, which is the most important concept in Dockerfile writing and nobody f*cking teaches it properly.

Here's the deal: Docker builds images layer by layer. If a layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses the cached version instead of rebuilding it. The `RUN pip install` layer depends on `requirements.txt`. If you copy your entire codebase first (including files that change constantly), Docker invalidates the cache and reinstalls all your pip packages every single time you change a single line of code. That's a 5-minute rebuild for a one-line fix. By copying `requirements.txt` separately and installing deps BEFORE copying the rest of the code, you cache the slow part and only rebuild when your dependencies actually change.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** I watched a junior dev at a startup rebuild a 2 GB image for 12 minutes every time he changed a comment in his code. Twelve. Minutes. Because he put `COPY . .` before `RUN pip install`. His company was paying for his coffee breaks while Docker reinstalled PyTorch 40 times a day. Don't be that guy.

**`RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt`** — Install Python dependencies. `--no-cache-dir` prevents pip from caching downloaded packages inside the image, which keeps it smaller. Every megabyte matters when you're pulling images.

**`COPY . .`** — NOW copy everything else. Your actual application code. This layer changes frequently, but because dependencies are already installed in a cached layer below, rebuilds are fast.

**`EXPOSE 8000`** — This is DOCUMENTATION. It does NOT actually open the port. It's a hint that says "hey, this container listens on 8000." You still need to publish the port with `-p` when running the container. I know, it's confusing. Docker's UX is garbage in places. Deal with it.

**`CMD ["python", "server.py"]`** — The default command to run when the container starts. This is the process that keeps the container alive. If this process dies, the container dies. Choose wisely.

### Port Mapping: The Bouncer at the Door

Your container has its own network with its own ports. The host machine can't see them by default. Port mapping is how you expose container ports to the outside world.

```bash
docker run -p 8080:8000 my-ai-app
```

This says: "Take port 8080 on the host and forward it to port 8000 in the container." Host port on the left, container port on the right. Think of it like a phone extension — the host is the main number, the container is the extension.

If your LLM runner listens on 8080 inside the container and you don't map it, you'll sit there like an idiot wondering why `curl localhost:8080` gives you connection refused. I've done this. Every Docker user has done this. Now you won't.

### Volume Binding: Make Data Survive

```bash
docker run -v model-cache:/app/models my-ai-app
```

This creates a named volume called `model-cache` and mounts it at `/app/models` inside the container. Your app writes model files there, and they persist across container restarts.

For bind mounts — when you want to point to a specific host directory:

```bash
docker run -v /home/you/models:/app/models my-ai-app
```

Host path on the left, container path on the right. The container reads and writes to `/app/models`, but the actual files live at `/home/you/models` on your server. You can edit them from the host, and the container sees the changes immediately.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Bind mounts can cause permission nightmares. If your container runs as root and writes files to a bind mount, those files are owned by root on the host. Then you try to edit them as your normal user and get `Permission denied`. Welcome to Docker's most common headache. Fix it by either running the container as your user (`--user $(id -u):$(id -g)`) or by setting proper permissions on the host directory beforehand.

### Environment Variables: Inject Config Without Rebuilding

```bash
docker run -e MODEL_NAME=llama3 -e GPU_LAYERS=35 my-ai-app
```

Environment variables are how you configure containers WITHOUT rebuilding the image. Change the model name? New env var. Adjust GPU layers? New env var. The image stays the same; only the runtime config changes. This is critical for your AI stack — you'll swap models constantly, and you do NOT want to rebuild a 5 GB image every time.

In a Dockerfile, you can set defaults:

```dockerfile
ENV MODEL_NAME=llama3
ENV GPU_LAYERS=35
```

Then override them at runtime with `-e`. The runtime value always wins. This is the Docker way — build once, configure at deployment.

### docker-compose.yml: Because Typing 40 Flags is for Idiots

Remember those `docker run` commands with fifteen flags? Now imagine managing five containers with interdependent configs. You'd need a shell script from hell just to start your stack. Enter Docker Compose — a YAML file that defines your entire multi-container setup.

```yaml
version: "3.9"

services:
  llm-backend:
    build: ./backend
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - model-cache:/app/models
    environment:
      - MODEL_NAME=llama3
      - GPU_LAYERS=35
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: 1
              capabilities: [gpu]

  frontend:
    build: ./frontend
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    depends_on:
      - llm-backend

volumes:
  model-cache:
```

Look at that. Clean. Readable. Reproducible. Everything in one file. You run `docker compose up -d` and the ENTIRE stack comes alive — backend, frontend, volumes, GPU access, network, everything. You run `docker compose down` and it all goes away cleanly. You share that file with someone, they run one command, and they have your exact same setup. No "works on my machine" bullsh*t.

Let me break down the critical parts:

**`services:`** — Each service is a container. `llm-backend` and `frontend` are just names you choose. These names become the hostnames on the Docker network — the frontend can reach the backend at `http://llm-backend:8080`.

**`deploy.resources.reservations.devices`** — This is how you give a container access to your GPU. That RTX 3090 you bought in Chapter 1? This is how Docker sees it. Without this block, your container is GPU-blind and your model runs on CPU at 2 tokens per second like it's 1997.

**`depends_on:`** — Tells Docker to start `llm-backend` before `frontend`. Note: this only controls STARTUP ORDER, not readiness. The frontend might start before the backend is actually ready to accept connections. In production, you handle this with healthchecks and retry logic. For now, just know the order is guaranteed.

**`volumes:` (top-level)** — This declares named volumes. The `model-cache:` at the bottom tells Docker "create and manage this volume." The `model-cache:/app/models` under the service tells Docker "mount it here."

### Debugging Like a Forensic Analyst

Your container just crashed. Welcome to Docker — where things break in ways that make you question your career choices. Here's your debugging toolkit:

**`docker logs <container>`** — The first thing you reach for. This shows you stdout and stderr from the container. If your app crashed, the error is here. Add `--tail 50` to see just the last 50 lines, or `-f` to follow logs in real-time. If you're running via Compose, use `docker compose logs -f` to see all services at once.

**`docker exec -it <container> bash`** — Drop into a running container with a shell. This is how you poke around when something's wrong. Check files, run commands, inspect the environment. If bash isn't available (slim images), try `sh`. This is your "I need to see what the hell is going on inside there" command.

**`docker inspect <container>`** — This dumps EVERYTHING Docker knows about the container in JSON format. Mounted volumes, network config, environment variables, exit codes, resource limits — everything. It's overwhelming, so pipe it through `jq` or grep for what you need: `docker inspect <container> | grep -A5 "State"`.

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** When a container exits immediately and you don't know why, check the exit code. `docker inspect <container> | grep ExitCode` tells you the code. Exit 0 means it finished successfully (maybe your CMD ran and exited). Exit 1 means a general error. Exit 137 means OOM — Docker killed it for using too much memory. That last one will save your ass when your model tries to load a 13B parameter quantized model into 8 GB of RAM.

Here's a debugging workflow that'll save you hours:

```bash
# What containers are running (or dead)?
docker ps -a

# What's the exit code?
docker inspect <container> | grep ExitCode

# What did it say before it died?
docker logs <container>

# Still alive but misbehaving? Get inside.
docker exec -it <container> bash

# Inside the container, check environment
env | grep MODEL

# Check if the GPU is visible
nvidia-smi

# Check if files are where you expect
ls -la /app/models/
```

Still breathing? Good. Because this next part separates the pretenders from the players.

### The Mental Model That Changes Everything

Here's what I need you to internalize, you dumb beautiful bastard. Docker is not about containers. It's about **reproducibility**. It's about the fact that six months from now, when you've forgotten every command, every config, every port number — you can spin up your entire AI stack from one `docker-compose.yml` file and it will work EXACTLY the way it does today.

Your OS (Chapter 2) is the foundation. Docker is the architecture. Every model you deploy, every frontend you bolt on, every database you wire up — it all lives inside containers now. Not scattered across your filesystem. Not installed via `apt` like some amateur. Contained. Isolated. Reproducible.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** The biggest mistake you'll make in the next week is getting lazy. You'll find some project on GitHub, see a `docker run` command, copy-paste it without understanding it, and move on. Don't. Read the Dockerfile. Understand what it's doing. Ask why each layer exists. That curiosity is what separates someone who runs Docker from someone who UNDERSTANDS Docker. Employers can tell the difference in about 30 seconds of an interview.

### The GPU Elephant in the Room

One thing Chapter 1's hardware doesn't help with if Docker can't see it: your GPU. You need the NVIDIA Container Toolkit installed on your HOST (not in the container — on the actual Ubuntu Server from Chapter 2). This is the bridge between your GPU drivers and Docker's container runtime.

```bash
# On your host (NOT in a container)
sudo apt-get install -y nvidia-container-toolkit
sudo systemctl restart docker
```

After that, test it:

```bash
docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi
```

If you see your GPU stats printed out, you're golden. If you see "could not select device driver" — your toolkit isn't installed or Docker wasn't restarted. Fix it before moving to Chapter 4, because the inference engines we're about to deploy will be USELESS without GPU access.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** The NVIDIA Container Toolkit works by hooking into Docker's runtime and injecting GPU device nodes into the container's namespace. It's not emulation — the container gets direct access to the GPU hardware, just with Docker's isolation layer around it. Performance overhead is effectively zero. Your model won't know it's in a container.

YOU ABSOLUTE LEGEND. You just understood Docker — images, containers, volumes, networks, Dockerfiles, Compose, debugging, GPU passthrough. Most people tap out somewhere around "what's a layer." Not you, you beautiful disaster. You're sitting here with a reproducible, containerized infrastructure foundation that's going to make every single chapter from here forward 10x easier.

Chapter 4 is where we load actual inference engines into these containers and make models talk. You've built the engine room. Now we're putting engines in it. Go install that NVIDIA Container Toolkit, verify your GPU shows up in a container, and meet me there.

modules.title: The Engine Room: Local LLM Runners
modules.content:
Picture this: you've got a beautiful RTX 3090 sitting in your server. Twenty-four gigs of VRAM. You've installed Ubuntu Server LTS like a pro, Docker is running clean, containers are humming. You feel like a god. Then you try to run a model and your terminal throws some error about "GGML tensor allocation failure" and your GPU sits there at 0% utilization like a Ferrari parked in a garage with no gas.

What the hell happened, champ? You forgot the engine. You built the chassis, wired the electrics, polished the paint — and never dropped the motor in. That's what this chapter is about. The inference engine. The thing that actually takes a model file and makes it TALK.

Without an inference engine, your model files are just multi-gigabyte paperweights sitting on a filesystem doing absolutely nothing. Like buying a Steinway piano and not knowing how to play Chopsticks. The model is the sheet music. The engine is the pianist. And right now, you don't have a pianist.

Let's fix that.

## Core Carnage (Rip Apart the Essentials)

### What the F*ck Is an Inference Engine, Actually?

Here's where 90% of people get it twisted. They think "local LLM" means one thing. It doesn't. There are TWO completely separate components:

1. **The Model** — a file (usually `.gguf`, `.safetensors`, or `.bin`) containing billions of numbers called "weights." These are the learned parameters. It's a frozen brain. It knows things but can't think on its own.

2. **The Inference Engine** — the software that loads those weights into VRAM, accepts your text prompt, runs the matrix math, and spits out tokens. It's the runtime. The animator. The thing that makes the dead brain alive.

You need BOTH. And the engine you pick determines everything — speed, compatibility, API surface, how many people can talk to your model at once, and whether your GPU actually does work or just watches from the sidelines.

### The Holy Trinity of Local Inference

There are three engines that matter right now. Not ten. Not "here's a top 10 list from some blog farm." Three. Anyone who tries to sell you on a fourth is either selling something or hasn't actually deployed in production.

---

#### 1. Ollama — The People's Champion

Ollama was built by the team behind Docker. Literally. Same DNA. And you can tell, because it does for LLMs what Docker did for deployment: it made the hard part disappear.

You want to run Llama 3? `ollama run llama3`. That's it. It pulls the model, quantizes it automatically, loads it into VRAM, gives you a chat prompt, AND spins up an OpenAI-compatible REST API on port 11434. No config file surgery. No Python environment hell. No "install these 47 dependencies and pray to the pip gods."

**Why it exists:** The founders looked at the state of local LLM tooling in 2023 — which was a dumpster fire of Jupyter notebooks and broken Conda environments — and said "this is stupid. We can fix this." They took the same philosophy that made Docker ubiquitous: hide the complexity, make it just work, give people a clean CLI and a clean API.

**What it's built on:** Under the hood, Ollama uses `llama.cpp` — the same C++ inference library that powers half the local AI ecosystem. It's not reinventing the math. It's wrapping the math in a package that doesn't make you want to die.

**The killer feature:** That OpenAI-compatible API. We're going to hammer on this later, but here's the short version: almost every AI tool, framework, and frontend on Earth knows how to talk to OpenAI's API format. Ollama speaks that same format natively. That means you can point almost any tool at Ollama and it just works. No adapters. No translation layers. No tears.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** Ollama isn't the fastest engine. It isn't the most configurable. It's the most USABLE. And in a world where you're juggling Docker, networking, storage, and security, usable wins. You can always optimize later. You can't optimize something you never got running.

---

#### 2. vLLM — The Speed Demon

vLLM came out of UC Berkeley's Sky Computing Lab. And it solved a problem most people didn't even know existed: memory waste.

See, when an LLM generates text, it has to keep track of something called the KV cache — the "memory" of what it's already generated so far in this conversation. Traditional engines allocated memory for this cache in big fixed chunks, like reserving an entire hotel room for every guest even if they just need a closet. The result? Your GPU memory was 60% empty but technically "full," and you couldn't serve more users even though you had the capacity.

vLLM introduced something called **PagedAttention** — and this is your mind-blown moment, so pay attention.

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** PagedAttention is literally borrowed from the operating system concept of virtual memory paging. You know how your OS doesn't need a program's entire memory to be physically contiguous? It breaks memory into 4KB pages and maps them wherever there's space? vLLM does the SAME THING with KV cache. It breaks the cache into small "pages" and scatters them across VRAM wherever there's room. This took KV cache memory waste from ~60% down to under 4%. It's the same trick your OS has used since the 1960s. They just applied it to LLMs. That's it. That's the innovation. And it DOUBLED throughput overnight.

**What vLLM is for:** High-concurrency serving. If you're the only person talking to your model, vLLM is overkill. But if you've got five people hitting the same model simultaneously, or you're running an automated pipeline that fires off 20 requests in parallel, vLLM doesn't just win — it makes the other engines look like they're standing still.

**The catch:** vLLM is a pain in the ass to configure. It expects specific model formats (HuggingFace safetensors, not GGUF). It has a billion command-line flags. It's built for production serving, not for "let me quickly test this model on my laptop." If Ollama is a Honda Civic — reliable, easy, starts every time — vLLM is a Formula 1 car. Faster, yes. But you need a pit crew.

---

#### 3. text-generation-webui (TGWUI) — The Tinkerer's Nightmare

Also known as "oobabooga" after its creator's GitHub username. This is the Swiss Army knife of local LLM tools. It supports EVERYTHING. GGUF, GPTQ, AWQ, EXL2, safetensors, bitsandbytes, llama.cpp, ExLlamaV2, AutoGPTQ — if it's a format or backend that exists, TGWUI probably has a checkbox for it.

**Why it exists:** Because sometimes you don't know what you need. You've got a weird model in a weird format and you just need SOMETHING that'll load it. TGWUI is that something.

**The problem:** It's a frontend strapped to a backend strapped to three different inference engines strapped to a Python environment that breaks if you look at it wrong. It's powerful but fragile. It's the "I have 14 tabs open and 7 of them are documentation" solution.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Using TGWUI as your production serving layer. Don't. It's a testing and experimentation tool. It's brilliant for "let me try this model in four different quantization formats and compare quality." It's a disaster for "I need this running 24/7 and my frontend needs to hit a stable API." Use it to learn. Use it to experiment. Then deploy with Ollama or vLLM.

---

### The Decision Matrix (Stop Overthinking It)

Here's your decision tree. Tattoo it on your forearm if you need to:

| Use Case | Engine | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Solo user, want it to "just work" | Ollama | Zero config, OpenAI API, Docker-friendly |
| Multiple concurrent users, need throughput | vLLM | PagedAttention, proper batching, production-grade |
| Experimenting with weird model formats | TGWUI | Supports everything, stable-ish, great for R&D |
| You want an API other tools can consume | Ollama or vLLM | Both speak OpenAI-compatible. TGWUI's API is janky. |

Still breathing? Good. Because this next part separates the pretenders from the players.

## Street Smarts (How to Actually Use This)

### Deploying Ollama in Docker with GPU Passthrough

You already set up Docker. You already dealt with GPU passthrough concepts in Module 1. Now we connect the dots.

First, make sure the NVIDIA Container Toolkit is installed on your host. This is the bridge that lets Docker containers access your GPU. Without it, your container is blind to the hardware.

```bash
# Add the NVIDIA package repository
curl -fsSL https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey \
  | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg

curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/stable/deb/nvidia-container-toolkit.list \
  | sed 's#deb https://#deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg] https://#g' \
  | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y nvidia-container-toolkit

# Configure Docker to use the NVIDIA runtime
sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker
sudo systemctl restart docker
```

Now verify your container can see the GPU:

```bash
docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.2.2-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi
```

If you see your GPU info table, you're golden. If you see "command not found" or "no GPU detected," go back and fix your toolkit installation. Do NOT proceed with a broken foundation. That's how you end up debugging at 3 AM wondering why your model is running on CPU at 2 tokens per second.

Now, the Ollama container. Here's your docker-compose.yml:

```yaml
services:
  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    container_name: ollama
    ports:
      - "11434:11434"
    volumes:
      - ollama_models:/root/.ollama
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all
              capabilities: [gpu]
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  ollama_models:
```

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** That `volumes` block is CRITICAL. Without it, every time you recreate the container, you lose all your downloaded models. We're talking 4-7 GB per model vanishing into the void. You'll re-download Llama 3 at 2 AM on a connection that caps at 20 Mbps and you'll contemplate violence. Mount the volume. Always.

Bring it up:

```bash
docker compose up -d
docker compose logs -f ollama
```

You should see Ollama start up and listen on port 11434. Now pull your first model:

```bash
# From inside the container
docker exec -it ollama ollama run llama3:8b
```

Or from the host using the API:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434/api/pull -d '{"name": "llama3:8b"}'
```

That first pull is going to take a minute. The 8B model in Q4 quantization is about 4.7 GB. Once it's down, you'll get a chat prompt. Type something. Anything. "Hello, you beautiful disaster."

It responds. YOU ABSOLUTE LEGEND. You just ran a local LLM. Most people tap out here. They read about it, they think about it, they never actually DO it. Not you. You beautiful disaster, you actually did the thing.

### The OpenAI-Compatible API — Your Secret Weapon

Here's where it gets real. Ollama isn't just a chat prompt. It's a full REST API. And it speaks the same language as OpenAI.

This is the part that makes your server useful to OTHER services. Remember — this is Module 4. In Module 6, you're going to bolt on a frontend. In Module 10, you're going to wire up RAG. ALL of those things need to talk to your model through an API. And almost every AI tool in existence knows how to talk to OpenAI's API format.

Ollama gives you that. For free. Out of the box.

Test it:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "llama3:8b",
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Why is my GPU so hot?"}],
    "temperature": 0.7
  }'
```

That endpoint — `/v1/chat/completions` — is the EXACT same endpoint format that OpenAI uses. Any tool that can talk to GPT-4 can talk to your local Llama 3. You just change the base URL from `https://api.openai.com` to `http://your-server:11434/v1`. That's it. No API key needed (yet — we'll lock that down in Module 8).

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** This is why Ollama is the default choice for home servers. It's not about raw speed. It's about COMPATIBILITY. The OpenAI-compatible API means your local model works with LangChain, LlamaIndex, Open WebUI, Dify, n8n, Continue.dev, and about a thousand other tools. You're not building a walled garden. You're building an interchange.

### Benchmarking: Know Your Damn Numbers

If you can't answer the question "how many tokens per second does my setup produce?" then you're flying blind. You don't know if your GPU is actually working. You don't know if that quantization setting helped or hurt. You don't know if you can serve two users or just one.

Here's a quick and dirty benchmark script:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# benchmark.sh — because guessing is for losers

MODEL="llama3:8b"
PROMPT="Explain quantum computing in exactly 200 words."

echo "Benchmarking $MODEL..."
echo "Prompt: $PROMPT"
echo "-----------------------------------"

START=$(date +%s.%N)

RESPONSE=$(curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/generate \
  -d "{
    \"model\": \"$MODEL\",
    \"prompt\": \"$PROMPT\",
    \"stream\": false
  }")

END=$(date +%s.%N)

ELAPSED=$(echo "$END - $START" | bc)
EVAL_COUNT=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq '.eval_count')
EVAL_DURATION=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq '.eval_duration')

# eval_duration is in nanoseconds, convert to seconds
EVAL_SECONDS=$(echo "scale=2; $EVAL_DURATION / 1000000000" | bc)
TPS=$(echo "scale=1; $EVAL_COUNT / $EVAL_SECONDS" | bc)

echo "Total time: ${ELAPSED}s"
echo "Tokens generated: $EVAL_COUNT"
echo "Generation time: ${EVAL_SECONDS}s"
echo "Tokens per second: $TPS"
echo "-----------------------------------"
```

Run that. Write down the number. Here's what you're looking for on common hardware:

| Hardware | Model | Quant | Expected TPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| RTX 3090 (24GB) | Llama 3 8B | Q4 | 80-110 |
| RTX 3090 (24GB) | Llama 3 70B | Q4 | 12-18 |
| RTX 4070 Ti (12GB) | Llama 3 8B | Q4 | 60-80 |
| RTX 3060 (12GB) | Llama 3 8B | Q4 | 35-50 |

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Comparing your TPS to cloud API speeds and getting discouraged. Stop. Cloud APIs run on $25,000 H100 clusters. You're running on a used GPU you bought off eBay. The point isn't to match OpenAI's latency. The point is to have a PRIVATE, FREE, UNCENSORED model that runs on YOUR hardware and doesn't send your data to anyone. If you're getting 40 TPS on Llama 3 8B, that's faster than reading speed. That's all you need for personal use.

### When to Abandon Ollama for vLLM

Ollama is your default. But there's a moment when you outgrow it. Here's how to know:

**You need vLLM when:**
- More than 3 people are hitting the same model simultaneously and Ollama starts queuing requests
- You need continuous batching (processing new requests while old ones are still generating)
- You're running a production service where latency variance matters
- You need to serve multiple models on the same GPU with smart memory management

**You do NOT need vLLM when:**
- It's just you and maybe your partner using the server
- You're testing models for fun
- You're building a RAG pipeline with low request volume
- You value your sanity and don't want to debug Python dependency hell

If you DO need vLLM, here's the absolute minimum Docker Compose to get it running:

```yaml
services:
  vllm:
    image: vllm/vllm-openai:latest
    container_name: vllm
    runtime: nvidia
    environment:
      - HUGGING_FACE_HUB_TOKEN=hf_your_token_here
    volumes:
      - vllm_cache:/root/.cache/huggingface
    command:
      - --model=meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct
      - --tensor-parallel-size=1
      - --max-model-len=8192
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    ipc: host
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  vllm_cache:
```

Note the differences. vLLM pulls from HuggingFace, not its own registry. It needs your HF token. It uses `runtime: nvidia` instead of the deploy block. And it has a TON of command-line flags that you will absolutely screw up on the first try. That's fine. That's normal. Read the docs, fix the flag, try again.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** I've seen people spend three days trying to make vLLM work when Ollama would have solved their problem in three minutes. Ego is the enemy here. You don't get bonus points for using the "advanced" tool. You get bonus points for shipping something that works. If Ollama handles your load, use Ollama. Move on with your life.

### The Model Registry Problem

One thing that trips up rookies: Ollama has its own model registry. You can't just point it at a random `.gguf` file on your hard drive — well, you CAN, but you need to create a "Modelfile" (Ollama's version of a Dockerfile) to do it:

```dockerfile
# Modelfile
FROM ./my-custom-model.gguf

PARAMETER temperature 0.7
PARAMETER context_length 8192

SYSTEM "You are a helpful assistant who speaks like a 1920s gangster."
```

Then:

```bash
docker exec -it ollama ollama create mymodel -f /path/to/Modelfile
```

This is how you load custom fine-tunes or models that aren't in Ollama's registry. It's also how you set system prompts and default parameters that persist across restarts.

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Always set `context_length` explicitly in your Modelfile. Ollama defaults to 2048 tokens, which is USELESS for real conversations. A single back-and-forth exchange will blow through that. Set it to 8192 minimum. If your VRAM can handle it, go 16384 or 32768. The model needs room to remember what the hell you were just talking about.

### Memory Management: The VRAM Budget

Here's the math that will save your life. Your VRAM is a budget. Everything costs something.

**Rule of thumb for VRAM consumption:**
- Model weights: model_size_in_GB × (quantization_factor)
  - Q4 (4-bit): roughly 0.5× the original size
  - Q8 (8-bit): roughly 1.0× the original size
  - FP16 (16-bit): roughly 2.0× the original size
- KV cache: ~0.5-1.5 GB per 1000 tokens of context (varies by model)
- Overhead: ~500MB-1GB for the engine itself

Example: Llama 3 8B in Q4 on a 12GB GPU:
- Weights: ~4.7 GB
- KV cache (8K context): ~1.5 GB
- Overhead: ~0.8 GB
- **Total: ~7 GB** — fits comfortably in 12GB, leaves room for the OS display server

Same model on an 8GB GPU:
- **Total: ~7 GB** — barely fits. One wrong move and you're swapping to system RAM, and your TPS drops from 60 to 3. Welcome to hell.
